title: Emergency Guide: Deep Gash on a Cat's Eyeball (Corneal Laceration)
meta_desc: A deep gash on your cat's eyeball is a critical medical emergency. Learn why immediate veterinary care is essential to save your pet's vision.
content_html:
What is a Corneal or Scleral Laceration?

A deep gash on a cat's eyeball is technically known as a corneal or scleral laceration. This occurs when a sharp object, such as a claw or a thorn, penetrates the outer layers of the eye. This type of injury is extremely painful and poses a direct threat to the internal structures of the eye, often leading to the leakage of intraocular fluid or the displacement of the iris.

Urgency Level: High Emergency

This condition is a high-level medical emergency. You should not wait for an appointment; seek the nearest emergency veterinary clinic immediately. Eye injuries progress rapidly, and a deep gash can lead to permanent blindness, severe infection (endophthalmitis), or the complete loss of the eye if not surgically addressed within hours.

How a Photo Helps Triage

In a triage situation, providing a clear, well-lit photo of the injury can be life-saving. A photo allows the veterinary team to assess the depth of the wound and check for signs of ocular collapse or protruding tissue before you even arrive. This helps the hospital prioritize your cat's care and prepare the necessary surgical equipment or specialists in advance.

faqs.question: Can I put human eye drops in my cat's eye?
faqs.answer: No. Never use human medications or over-the-counter drops on a ruptured eye. Many ingredients are toxic to the internal chambers of the eye and can worsen the damage.

faqs.question: How can I protect the eye on the way to the vet?
faqs.answer: The most important step is to prevent your cat from scratching or rubbing the eye. Place an Elizabethan collar (cone) on them immediately. If you do not have one, keep their hands gently restrained or wrapped in a towel.

faqs.question: Will my cat lose their eye?
faqs.answer: While a deep gash is serious, many eyes can be saved with prompt microsurgery. However, if the injury is too extensive or becomes infected, surgical removal (enucleation) may be necessary to prevent chronic pain.
